items.fix: Identify which event is the evidence (condition) and which is the hypothesis. P(A|B) uses P(B|A)P(A)/P(B); swapping them without justification is the prosecutor's fallacy.
items.why: P(B|A) and P(A|B) differ unless P(A) = P(B); treating them as interchangeable leads to wildly wrong decisions in medicine, law, and ML triage.
items.example: A test with P(+|disease)=0.99 does **not** mean P(disease|+)=0.99 when the disease is rare—always compute the posterior with Bayes.

items.mistake: Forgetting to compute P(B) via total probability
items.fix: Expand P(B) = P(B|A)P(A) + P(B|Aᶜ)P(Aᶜ) (or sum over a full partition) before dividing.
items.why: Using P(B) = P(B|A) alone ignores alternative paths to B and inflates the posterior.
items.example: With P(S)=0.01 and P(F|S)=0.90, setting P(F)=0.90 gives P(S|F)=0.01—wrong. Correct P(F)≈0.0585 gives P(S|F)≈0.154.

items.fix: Check P(B) > 0 before applying Bayes; if B is impossible, conditioning on B is undefined.
items.why: Conditional probability P(A|B) requires P(B) > 0 by definition; Bayes' theorem inherits this requirement.
items.example: If a model assigns P(flag)=0 for all inputs, P(spam|flag) is undefined—not zero.

items.mistake: Treating the likelihood P(B|A) as a probability distribution over A
items.fix: Normalize across hypotheses: P(Aᵢ|B) = P(B|Aᵢ)P(Aᵢ) / Σⱼ P(B|Aⱼ)P(Aⱼ). Likelihoods over A need not sum to 1.
items.why: Likelihood measures compatibility of data with each hypothesis; only after multiplying by priors and dividing by evidence do you get a valid posterior.
items.example: Likelihoods [0.1, 0.2] for two coins do not mean P(coin₂|data)=0.67 until priors and P(data) are applied.
